common_name: Flordaking Peach (University of Florida Low-Chill Early Freestone Peach for Zone 9)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant; Flordaking requires only 250–350 chilling hours (hours below 45°F), making it one of the lowest-chill peach varieties adaptable to zone 9 conditions where winters are mild. In zone 9 (Gulf Coast region), the standard 400–500 chill hours required by most standard peach varieties are often not accumulated, leading to poor bloom and production; Flordaking's low chill requirement allows reliable production in zone 9. The fruit ripens in May–June — relatively early in the peach season — before summer heat becomes intense. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage; peach trees are susceptible to root rot in poorly drained soils. Flordaking is self-fertile and does not require a cross-pollinator, though cross-pollination with another early-blooming peach may increase fruit set.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; prune to an open-vase shape in year 1. Thin fruit clusters to 6–8 inches between fruits when marble-sized. Flordaking Peach was released by the University of Florida Tropical Research and Education Center (then Florida Agricultural Experiment Station) in 1957 as one of the pioneering low-chill peach varieties specifically bred for Florida and Gulf Coast conditions. The development of low-chill peach varieties began in earnest at the University of Florida under the leadership of George Lyrene and others who recognized that the warm-winter climate of Florida and the Gulf Coast made it impossible to grow standard peach varieties requiring 600–800+ chilling hours. Flordaking became one of the landmark early low-chill releases: a freestone variety (the pit separates easily from the flesh at full ripeness) with medium-large fruit, yellow skin blushed with red, and yellow flesh of good eating quality for a low-chill variety. The variety's reputation for reliability in low-chill conditions — producing consistent crops when other peach varieties bloom prematurely or fail to set properly in zone 9's mild winters — established Flordaking as a foundation variety in Gulf Coast peach production, and it remains planted and recommended decades after its introduction. For zone 9 gardeners, Flordaking represents one of the most accessible routes to growing tree-ripe peaches at home in a climate where most stone fruits are impractical.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Flordaking peach variety development and low-chill peach production
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Low-Chill Peach Variety Recommendations for the Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Peach Variety Selection for Texas and the Gulf Coast

common_name: Ruston Red Peach (Louisiana Heirloom Clingstone Peach for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; Ruston Red requires approximately 400–450 chilling hours — more than low-chill varieties like Flordaking, but still within reach of most zone 9 locations in Louisiana, Mississippi, and East Texas where winters accumulate sufficient chill. In years with warm winters, Ruston Red may bloom poorly; in years with adequate chill, it produces reliably. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun. The variety produces fruit in June–July in zone 9, slightly later than very early low-chill peaches, and the fruit keeps better on the tree and post-harvest than very early types. Ruston Red is particularly well-adapted to the humidity of the Gulf South and is considered one of the better locally-adapted peach varieties for the Midsouth region.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in full sun, well-drained soil. Thin fruit when marble-sized to 6–8 inches apart. Ruston Red is an heirloom peach variety associated with Ruston, Louisiana — a city in Lincoln Parish in north Louisiana that became famous for its peaches and has celebrated an annual Peach Festival since 1951. Ruston is located in a sweet spot for peach production in Louisiana: elevated enough above the Gulf Coast to accumulate adequate winter chill hours for most peach varieties, yet with a long enough warm season for fruit development, making the area one of the most productive peach regions in the Deep South. Ruston Red is a clingstone variety (the flesh adheres to the pit, making fresh eating more effortful but canning and preserving easier), with deep red skin and yellow to orange flesh, good sweetness, and the rich peach flavor associated with tree-ripened southern peaches. The variety has been grown in Louisiana for multiple generations and represents the type of locally-adapted, regionally-specific heirloom fruit variety that is increasingly important to preserve as commercial agriculture standardizes toward nationally-marketed varieties. Ruston Red's reputation in Louisiana is one of strong local association and pride: the name carries the flavor of place, as fruit varieties often do in American agricultural tradition, linking the eating experience to a specific region's history and soil.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Peach Variety Production for North Louisiana
Ruston Peach Festival Historical Documentation — Ruston, Louisiana peach heritage
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Heritage peach variety documentation for the Southeast

common_name: Burgundy Plum (Small Dark-Red Japanese Plum for Zone 9, Very Low Chill Requirement)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter; Burgundy requires approximately 200–250 chilling hours — among the lowest chill requirements of any plum variety — making it one of the most reliable Japanese plum choices for zone 9 conditions where many standard plums (requiring 500–800 chill hours) fail to bloom and produce reliably. Plant with a cross-pollinator (Santa Rosa, Methley, or Beauty plum) within 50 feet for best fruit set, as Burgundy benefits significantly from cross-pollination. Burgundy ripens June–July in zone 9, producing small to medium fruits with deep red-burgundy skin. The compact tree size (smaller than most Japanese plum types) makes Burgundy practical for smaller home gardens where a standard-sized plum would be impractically large. Excellent for Gulf Coast gardens where low-chill performance is essential.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in full sun; requires cross-pollinator. Thin fruit clusters to 3–4 inches when marble-sized. Harvest when deep burgundy-red and slightly soft. Burgundy Plum is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) variety originating in California that has become one of the most valued low-chill plum varieties for gardeners in zones 8–10 where the winter chilling hours required by standard plum varieties are inconsistently accumulated. The variety takes its name from the fruit's distinctive deep red to burgundy-purple skin color — one of the darkest red Japanese plum varieties — which covers a sweet, amber-yellow flesh with good sweetness and mild plum flavor. The fruit is smaller than most Japanese plum types (typically 1.5–2 inches) but produced prolifically in good crop years, and the deep color makes Burgundy one of the most visually attractive plums when displayed at market or in fresh fruit arrangements. California agriculture recognized the low-chill potential of Burgundy and similar varieties in the warm Central Valley and southern coastal regions, leading to its adoption as a commercial variety in some California districts and its recommendation by cooperative extension services in low-chill regions nationwide. For Gulf Coast and Gulf South gardeners in zone 9, Burgundy represents one of the few plum varieties that can be grown reliably without the risk of insufficient chilling — a real advantage in an area where most stone fruit production is challenging.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Low-Chill Plum Variety Descriptions
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Plum Variety Selection for Texas and the Gulf Coast
Dave Wilson Nursery — Burgundy plum low-chill commercial variety notes

common_name: Kanza Pecan (USDA Scab-Resistant Mid-Season Pecan, Excellent for the Midsouth)

planting.note: Plant grafted nursery trees in late winter while dormant; Kanza is a Type I (protandrous) pecan that releases pollen before the pistillate flowers are receptive on the same tree, requiring a Type II pecan pollinator within 150 feet for reliable fertilization and full crop. Pair Kanza with Pawnee, Oconee, or Desirable (all Type II) for best results. In zone 9, pecan trees require deep, well-drained, fertile soil with adequate zinc availability — zinc deficiency is common in Gulf South pecan trees and causes rosette symptoms; apply zinc sulfate foliar sprays as recommended by extension guidelines. Trees begin significant production 5–8 years after planting and remain productive for 50–100+ years. Annual pruning to maintain open canopy structure improves light penetration and air circulation — critical for scab management even in a resistant variety.

directions: Plant grafted trees in deep, well-drained soil; apply zinc sulfate foliar sprays annually. Pair with a Type II pollinator within 150 feet. Kanza Pecan was released by the USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) Pecan Breeding Program in 1984 as part of the Northern Pecan Testing Program — a cooperative breeding effort between the USDA and several state agricultural experiment stations to develop superior pecan varieties adapted to a wide range of conditions from the Gulf Coast northward through Oklahoma, Kansas, and Missouri. Kanza is notable for its excellent resistance to pecan scab (Fusicladium effusum), the most economically damaging disease of pecans in the humid Gulf South region, where high summer humidity and rainfall create ideal conditions for scab development on susceptible varieties. Standard commercial varieties like Desirable and Stuart have significant scab susceptibility and require intensive fungicide programs in humid regions; Kanza's improved scab resistance significantly reduces the fungicide inputs required for commercial-quality nut production. The nuts are medium-sized with good kernel quality and relatively thin shells, and they mature in mid-season — earlier than some standard varieties — allowing harvest before fall rains that can cause nut deterioration. For Gulf South pecan growers (backyard and small-scale), Kanza's disease resistance reduces management burden substantially compared to growing standard susceptible varieties.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Kanza pecan release notes and performance data
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pecan Variety Recommendations for Louisiana
Oklahoma State University Extension — Pecan Variety Selection and Management Guide

common_name: Narrowleaf Zinnia (Species Zinnia, Drought and Mildew Resistant Orange Annual)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Zinnia angustifolia is the most mildew-resistant and drought-tolerant of the garden zinnia species, making it significantly more practical in hot, humid zone 9 conditions than the large-flowered Zinnia elegans types that often develop powdery mildew by mid-summer. In zone 9, plant April–June for summer bloom and again August for fall. Narrowleaf Zinnia forms a spreading mound 8–12 inches tall that provides excellent ground coverage in hot, dry conditions — planting closely (8–10 inches) creates a dense carpet of orange flowers. The species is the wild parent of the interspecific Profusion series hybrids (Zinnia marylandica = Z. angustifolia × Z. elegans), which have become among the most popular summer annuals; the species itself is worth growing for its simplicity, easy direct sowing, and exceptional heat and drought resistance.

directions: Scatter seeds on prepared soil after last frost; barely cover with soil. Narrowleaf Zinnia flowers prolifically through the hottest part of summer with minimal deadheading. Zinnia angustifolia (narrowleaf zinnia, spreading zinnia, or Mexican zinnia) is a small-flowered species zinnia native to Mexico and Central America that differs substantially from the large-flowered, upright garden zinnia (Zinnia elegans) in plant habit, flower size, and growing conditions. The leaves are distinctively narrow (hence the common name) compared to the broad, ovate leaves of Z. elegans, and the plant forms a low, spreading mound rather than upright stems. The flowers are small (1–1.5 inches), single, with a simple daisy form in bright orange — a pure, vivid orange that is more intense than many of the blended or muted colors of Z. elegans varieties. The most important practical distinction is disease resistance: Zinnia angustifolia has significant genetic resistance to powdery mildew, the fungal disease that devastates Zinnia elegans plantings in hot, humid climates by mid-summer, leaving plants disfigured and non-productive. In the Gulf South and other hot-humid regions, narrowleaf zinnia is a more reliable long-season annual than Z. elegans because it continues producing flowers through August and September without the mildew damage that typically limits Z. elegans performance. Plant breeders recognized this and created the Profusion zinnia series (Z. marylandica) by crossing Z. angustifolia with Z. elegans to combine the disease resistance of the former with the larger flowers and color range of the latter.
sources: All-America Selections — Zinnia angustifolia species evaluation for heat and mildew resistance
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Narrowleaf vs. garden zinnia species comparison
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flower Selection for Hot-Humid Climates
